pattern: Reduce every string to the set of distinct letters it uses (order and repetition don't matter), then group words by that signature and count how many ways to pick two words from each group.
hints: Two strings are similar exactly when they use the same set of distinct characters — repeats and order inside the string don't matter at all.
Turn each word into a canonical signature that captures only 'which letters appear' (a sorted tuple of the distinct letters, or a 26-bit mask both work) and group words by that signature.
Within a group of size c, every pair counts, and pairs across different groups never do — so the answer is the sum over groups of c*(c-1)/2.

constraints.note: each word length 1..100, lowercase English letters only

edge_inputs.description: two words with identical letter sets but different lengths and repeats
no two words share the same letter set
all words share one common letter set — every pair counts
single-character words grouped by which single letter they use
shortest possible words, all length 1, split across two letter groups
a word using all 26 letters, so it can never match a shorter word's set
same multiset of letter counts is not the same as same letter set — words with equal length and equal letter frequencies but one extra distinct letter must NOT be paired
a word repeating one letter many times must still be considered similar to a word using each of those same distinct letters just once

stress.description: 100 random words built from a small alphabet so many end up sharing a letter set
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    import string
    n = max(1, size)
    alphabet = string.ascii_lowercase[:6]
    words = []
    for _ in range(n):
        k = rng.randint(1, 4)
        letters = rng.sample(alphabet, k)
        length = rng.randint(k, 20)
        word = [rng.choice(letters) for _ in range(length)]
        rng.shuffle(word)
        words.append(''.join(word))
    return [words]
